questions.question: What is the key difference between linear and affine usage?
options: Linear requires exactly once; affine allows at most once
questions.explanation: Linear enforces exact consumption; affine permits dropping but not duplication.

questions.question: Why do linear types prevent memory leaks in theory?
options: Every allocated resource must be consumed exactly once, preventing forgotten cleanup
questions.explanation: By requiring exactly one use, linear types ensure resources are always explicitly consumed (e.g., freed or closed).

questions.question: How does Rust ownership relate to affine typing intuition?
options: Values are freely duplicated by default
Moves prevent uncontrolled reuse, approximating affine constraints
questions.explanation: Rust move semantics and borrow rules enforce disciplined resource usage with compile-time checks.

questions.question: Why are these constraints useful for systems code?
options: They reduce resource-lifecycle and aliasing bug classes
questions.explanation: Ownership disciplines prevent many misuse patterns around memory and resources.

questions.question: What is the `Drop` trait in Rust an example of?
options: Affine type enforcement: values are consumed (dropped) when they go out of scope
questions.explanation: `Drop` ensures cleanup code runs when an affine value is consumed at scope end, combining ownership discipline with deterministic destruction.

_meta.title: Linear and Affine Types - Rust's Lineage
